subject: Governance
trap_factor: Option A (SHGs) and C (adolescent girls) are plausible because SWAYAM sounds like a women/rural empowerment scheme. The name misleads candidates away from the e-learning context.
elimination_hint: SWAYAM stands for Study Webs of Active-Learning for Young Aspiring Minds — the name itself indicates an education/learning platform, pointing directly to option D.
factual_hooks: SWAYAM is India's MOOC platform offering free online courses
Launched under Digital India for affordable quality education
contrast_with: SWAYAM vs Skill India — education platform vs skill development
contemporary_relevance: SWAYAM remains central to India's online education strategy post-COVID.

subject: History
trap_factor: Options A and B (social/educational reforms) are tempting as Montagu-Chelmsford era did witness socio-educational changes, but the proposals were specifically about constitutional/political reforms.
elimination_hint: Montagu-Chelmsford Report 1918 led to the Government of India Act 1919 introducing dyarchy — it was about constitutional/political reform, eliminating all other options.
factual_hooks: Montagu-Chelmsford Proposals of 1918 led to GoI Act 1919
Introduced dyarchy at provincial level
contrast_with: Morley-Minto Reforms 1909 — introduced separate electorates vs Montagu-Chelmsford introducing dyarchy

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1 is a strong trap — candidates may think both are ancient rock-cut sites from a similar era, but Ajanta is Buddhist (2nd century BCE–5th CE) while Mahabalipuram is Hindu/Pallava (7th–8th century CE).
elimination_hint: Both are rock-cut monuments (statement 3 is correct). Statement 1 is false — different periods. Statement 2 is false — Ajanta is Buddhist, Mahabalipuram is Hindu. Answer is B (3 only).
factual_hooks: Ajanta is Buddhist rock-cut (2nd BCE–5th CE); Mahabalipuram is Hindu Pallava (7th–8th CE)
Both are UNESCO World Heritage Sites with rock-cut architecture
contrast_with: Ajanta (Buddhist) vs Ellora (Buddhist+Hindu+Jain) — religious denomination difference

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the classic trap — Bitcoin operates on decentralized blockchain; no central bank tracks or controls it. Candidates may confuse regulatory interest with actual tracking.
elimination_hint: Bitcoin is decentralized and untracked by central banks (S1 false). S2 and S3 are core features of Bitcoin — peer-to-peer and pseudonymous transactions. Answer: B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Bitcoin uses decentralized blockchain — no central bank oversight
Bitcoin transactions are pseudonymous, not anonymous
contrast_with: Bitcoin (decentralized) vs CBDC (central bank controlled)
contemporary_relevance: Cryptocurrency regulation remains a live debate in India and globally.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the trap — NDB was set up by BRICS nations (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa), not APEC. Candidates mixing up multilateral development banks easily pick option C.
elimination_hint: NDB was established by BRICS at the 2014 Fortaleza summit, not APEC. Statement 2 (HQ in Shanghai) is correct. Eliminate S1, answer is B.
factual_hooks: New Development Bank founded by BRICS in 2014 with HQ in Shanghai
NDB focuses on infrastructure and sustainable development in emerging economies
contrast_with: NDB (BRICS) vs AIIB (China-led) — different membership and mandate
contemporary_relevance: NDB expanding membership and role in global development finance.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Options A (constitutional reforms) and B (Ganga) may confuse candidates who recall these committees in a water/governance context; Kasturirangan is also linked to NEP which further confuses.
elimination_hint: Both Gadgil and Kasturirangan committees were specifically constituted to study ecology and conservation of the Western Ghats biodiversity hotspot — option D.
factual_hooks: Gadgil report (2011) recommended making 64% of Western Ghats ESZ; Kasturirangan (2013) reduced it to 37%
Western Ghats is a UNESCO World Heritage biodiversity hotspot
contrast_with: Gadgil (stricter conservation) vs Kasturirangan (moderate approach) for Western Ghats
contemporary_relevance: Western Ghats ESZ notification remains contentious in Kerala and Karnataka.

subject: History
trap_factor: The Calcutta Unitarian Committee (item 1) is tricky — it was associated with Ram Mohan Roy/Unitarian connections, not Keshab Chandra Sen. Candidates may credit Sen with all Brahmo-related bodies.
elimination_hint: Keshab Chandra Sen founded the Church of New Dispensation (Nababidhan) and Indian Reform Association. The Calcutta Unitarian Committee predates him and is linked to Ram Mohan Roy. Answer: B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Keshab Chandra Sen founded Nababidhan (Church of New Dispensation) in 1878
Indian Reform Association (1870) promoted social reforms including women's education
contrast_with: Debendranath Tagore (Adi Brahmo) vs Keshab Chandra Sen (progressive wing)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Iran is geographically in the Gulf region and a major oil power, making it a very plausible GCC member for uninformed candidates. Its exclusion is due to political/sectarian reasons.
elimination_hint: GCC was formed in 1981 by six Arab monarchies: Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Oman. Iran (non-Arab, Shia-majority) was never a member. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: GCC comprises 6 Sunni Arab monarchies; Iran excluded due to sectarian/political rivalry
GCC formed in 1981 in Abu Dhabi
contrast_with: GCC (Arab Sunni monarchies) vs OPEC (broader oil producers including Iran)
contemporary_relevance: Iran-Saudi normalization talks affect Gulf regional dynamics.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 2 (FDI promotion) is the trap — these schemes are about monetizing domestic gold holdings and reducing import dependence, not about attracting foreign investment into jewellery.
elimination_hint: Both schemes aim to mobilize idle household gold (S1) and cut import bills (S3). FDI in gold sector (S2) is unrelated to these domestic mobilization schemes. Answer: C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: India holds ~25,000 tonnes of household gold — world's largest private holding
Gold Monetization Scheme offers interest on deposited gold; Sovereign Gold Bonds substitute physical gold
contrast_with: Sovereign Gold Bond (paper gold with returns) vs Gold Monetization Scheme (deposit physical gold)
contemporary_relevance: Gold import management remains critical to India's CAD management.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: No significant trap — this was in news clearly associated with China's infrastructure diplomacy. Candidates may hesitate between EU and China if unaware.
elimination_hint: Belt and Road Initiative (BRI/OBOR) is China's flagship global infrastructure connectivity program launched by Xi Jinping in 2013. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: BRI (Belt and Road Initiative) involves 150+ countries and trillions in investment
India has not joined BRI citing sovereignty concerns over CPEC
contrast_with: BRI (China-led) vs G7 PGII (Western alternative infrastructure initiative)
contemporary_relevance: BRI expansion and India's strategic concerns remain highly relevant.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option B (loans for farmers) is tempting as MUDRA sounds like a rural/agricultural scheme; option D (skill development) is also plausible given government's skill push.
elimination_hint: MUDRA = Micro Units Development and Refinance Agency — specifically targets small/micro entrepreneurs for credit access through formal banking channels. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: PMMY provides loans up to ₹10 lakh under Shishu, Kishor, Tarun categories
Aims to bring unbanked micro-entrepreneurs into formal credit system
contrast_with: MUDRA (micro-enterprise credit) vs KCC (Kisan Credit Card for farmers)
contemporary_relevance: MUDRA loans play key role in supporting informal sector entrepreneurship.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may be uncertain about Cambay Basin's shale potential as it is primarily known for conventional oil/gas. All three basins actually have shale gas potential.
elimination_hint: India's shale gas resources are found across multiple sedimentary basins including Cambay (Gujarat), Cauvery (TN), and Krishna-Godavari (AP/Telangana). Answer: D (all three).
factual_hooks: India's shale gas potential is primarily in Cambay, KG Basin, and Cauvery Basin
India's shale gas exploration is at nascent stage unlike USA
contrast_with: Shale gas (unconventional, hydraulic fracturing) vs conventional natural gas
contemporary_relevance: Shale gas policy linked to India's energy independence strategy.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: World Bank (IBRD) and OECD are plausible distractors as they also publish major economic reports. Candidates may confuse IMF's financial stability focus with World Bank's development focus.
elimination_hint: IMF specifically monitors global financial system health and publishes the Global Financial Stability Report (GFSR) biannually. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: IMF's GFSR assesses global financial system risks and vulnerabilities
Published twice yearly (April and October)
contrast_with: GFSR (IMF — financial stability) vs World Development Report (World Bank — development issues)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 2 (only one family member) and Statement 3 (same pension for spouse) are both tricky — APY allows spouse continuation but at same guaranteed amount, while family restriction is not strict.
elimination_hint: APY guarantees minimum pension for unorganized workers (S1 correct). Spouse receives same guaranteed pension after subscriber's death (S3 correct). S2 is incorrect — both spouses can join. Answer: C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: APY guarantees pension of ₹1000–₹5000/month at age 60 for unorganized sector
Spouse continues to receive same pension after subscriber's death
contrast_with: APY (guaranteed pension) vs NPS (market-linked pension)
contemporary_relevance: Social security coverage for informal workers is a critical policy challenge.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: G20 is a plausible answer since RCEP involves large economies; SAARC may be chosen by candidates who associate it with regional trade in South/SE Asia.
elimination_hint: RCEP is the world's largest free trade agreement involving ASEAN-10 plus dialogue partners. It is directly negotiated within the ASEAN framework. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: RCEP signed in 2020 includes ASEAN+5 (China, Japan, South Korea, Australia, NZ)
India withdrew from RCEP negotiations in 2019
contrast_with: RCEP (ASEAN+5) vs CPTPP (US-led initially, now without US)
contemporary_relevance: India's reconsideration of RCEP membership remains a live trade policy debate.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Candidates may be unsure whether tubular fluorescent lamps fall under BEE star labeling as LED bulbs are more associated with energy ratings today. Ceiling fans were added later.
elimination_hint: BEE Star Label program covers ceiling fans, geysers, and fluorescent lamps — all are high energy consumers in Indian households. Answer: D (all three).
factual_hooks: BEE Star Label rates appliances 1–5 stars for energy efficiency
Launched by Ministry of Power under Energy Conservation Act 2001
contrast_with: BEE Star Label (mandatory/voluntary rating) vs ISO certification (quality standard)
contemporary_relevance: Energy efficiency labeling critical for India's climate commitments.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Option A (thorium) is a classic India-specific trap — India has thorium reserves and ITER relates to nuclear, tempting candidates to link the two. But ITER is about fusion, not thorium fission.
elimination_hint: ITER is an international nuclear fusion experiment. Success would enable fusion reactors for power generation — the immediate advantage for India. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: ITER (International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor) aims to demonstrate commercial fusion power
Fusion uses hydrogen isotopes (deuterium/tritium), not uranium/thorium
contrast_with: Fusion (ITER, clean, limitless fuel) vs Fission (current reactors, nuclear waste issues)
contemporary_relevance: Fusion energy progress (including private ventures) increasingly relevant to energy future.

subject: History
trap_factor: All four options are rulers of later Vijayanagar period and candidates must know the precise chronological overlap. Venkata II and Ranga II are both plausible late Vijayanagar rulers.
elimination_hint: Raja Wodeyar founded Mysore Kingdom circa 1399 CE. The then ruler of Vijayanagar was Venkata II (late 16th–early 17th century is slightly off — context suggests Venkata I era). Standard answer is D (Venkata II).
factual_hooks: Mysore Kingdom founded by Yaduraya (Raja Wodeyar) around 1399
Vijayanagar Empire ruled by Sangama, Saluva, Tuluva, Aravidu dynasties in succession
contrast_with: Tuluva dynasty (peak of Vijayanagar) vs Aravidu dynasty (decline period)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 2 (Taniyurs = Brahmin donation villages) is the trap — these were called Brahmadeya or Agrahara, not Taniyurs. Taniyurs were actually autonomous self-governing villages.
elimination_hint: Eripatti (tank-maintenance land) and Ghatikas (temple colleges) are correctly described. Taniyur refers to independent/autonomous villages, not Brahmin donation villages. Answer: D (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Eripatti: revenue reserved for village tank maintenance in Chola records
Ghatikas were Sanskrit colleges attached to temples in South India
contrast_with: Taniyur (autonomous village) vs Brahmadeya (Brahmin grant village)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Option A (refugee crisis agency) is tempting given the 2015–16 European refugee crisis context when this question was set. Option D (conflict resolution) is also plausible.
elimination_hint: European Stability Mechanism (ESM) was created in 2012 to provide financial assistance to eurozone countries facing debt crises (e.g., Greece bailout). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: ESM established 2012 with €700 billion capacity to bail out eurozone members
Created in response to European sovereign debt crisis
contrast_with: ESM (financial stability fund) vs ECB (monetary policy) — different EU financial mechanisms

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 2 might be doubted as candidates wonder if location-independent access is truly guaranteed given internet dependency, but this is a stated feature of the system.
elimination_hint: DigiLocker is a government-run digital document wallet under Digital India (S1 correct) allowing access from anywhere (S2 correct). Answer: C (Both).
factual_hooks: DigiLocker stores issued documents digitally eliminating need for physical copies
Partners with 1500+ issuers including RTO, education boards, government agencies
contrast_with: DigiLocker (government digital wallet) vs private cloud storage — official verification vs personal storage
contemporary_relevance: DigiLocker increasingly used for driving licences, marksheets as legally valid documents.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Cauvery-Tungabhadra link seems plausible given both are South Indian rivers. Mahanadi-Sone is another possible confusion point.
elimination_hint: The Godavari-Krishna link (Pattiseema project) was completed and water was transferred for the first time in 2015. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Pattiseema lift irrigation scheme links Godavari and Krishna rivers in Andhra Pradesh
First inter-linking project completed in India
contrast_with: Godavari-Krishna (completed link) vs Ken-Betwa (upcoming first Himalayan rivers link)
contemporary_relevance: River interlinking remains a major water management policy debate.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: CO2 is the most talked-about greenhouse gas, leading many to include it. Methane is another greenhouse gas often in news. But AQI specifically excludes CO2 and CH4 as they are not direct pollutants measurable in AQI.
elimination_hint: India's AQI measures PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb — not CO2 or methane (greenhouse gases, not criteria pollutants). Answer: B (CO, NO2, SO2).
factual_hooks: India's AQI uses 8 pollutants: PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb
CO2 and CH4 are greenhouse gases measured separately — not part of AQI
contrast_with: AQI pollutants (health impact) vs greenhouse gases (climate impact) — different measurement frameworks
contemporary_relevance: Air quality monitoring critical given Delhi/urban pollution crisis.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the trap — ESA, Japan (ASTRO-H), and Canada also launched space observatories before Astrosat. Statement 2 about orbital parameters may also be misremembered.
elimination_hint: Astrosat (2015) was not the only non-US/Russia observatory — ESA had XMM-Newton. S2 is also wrong on the exact orbital altitude (~650 km, not 1650 km). Answer: D (neither).
factual_hooks: Astrosat launched September 2015 — India's first dedicated multi-wavelength space observatory
Orbits at ~650 km altitude, not 1650 km as stated in option
contrast_with: Astrosat (multi-wavelength) vs Hubble (optical/UV only)

subject: History
trap_factor: Option A (bonded labour) and B (land grants to military officers) are common medieval terms that may seem related to the feudal economic context.
elimination_hint: Araghatta is a Persian-origin term for a water-lifting device (waterwheel) used in irrigation during the medieval period. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Araghatta was a Persian waterwheel (animal-powered) used for lifting water for irrigation
Common in North Indian medieval agriculture during Sultanate period
contrast_with: Araghatta (waterwheel) vs Noria (continuous chain of pots) — irrigation device types

subject: History
trap_factor: Shramana and Parivraajaka both suggest wandering/renunciant traditions associated with storytelling; Agrahaarika sounds like a learned/literate person who could preserve chronicles.
elimination_hint: Maagadha (also called Magadha/Suta) were the professional bards/chroniclers in ancient India who memorized and recited dynastic histories and epics. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Maagadhas were the professional bards/chroniclers in ancient India maintaining oral tradition
Sutras/Sutas were also associated with this tradition of oral history preservation
contrast_with: Shramana (renunciant ascetic) vs Maagadha (professional court bard/chronicler)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Arunachal Pradesh and Himachal Pradesh are associated with butterfly biodiversity/state symbols making them plausible; Karnataka is another butterfly-rich state.
elimination_hint: Maharashtra was the first Indian state to declare a State Butterfly — the Blue Mormon (Papilio polymnestor) in 2015. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Maharashtra declared Blue Mormon as State Butterfly in 2015 — first in India
Blue Mormon is one of the largest butterflies found in India
contrast_with: State Butterfly (Maharashtra first) vs State Animal (each state has different ones)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 is the trap — candidates may think India was second after USA, forgetting Soviet Union/Russia also reached Mars. Statement 1 (also called MOM) is correct.
elimination_hint: MOM = Mars Orbiter Mission (S1 correct). India was the 4th entity (after USSR, USA, ESA) but first Asian nation, and FIRST to succeed in maiden attempt (S3 correct). S2 is wrong. Answer: C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: MOM/Mangalyaan launched Nov 2013, entered Mars orbit Sept 2014
India is the only country to succeed in Mars orbit insertion on its very first attempt
contrast_with: India (first attempt success) vs China (Yinghuo-1 failed) in Mars missions

subject: History
trap_factor: Option A (Minto communalism) is tempting as communal politics was indeed used by British, and Muslim League founding (C) was contemporaneous. But the direct cause of Surat Split was ideological within Congress.
elimination_hint: The Surat Split of 1907 was caused by the Extremists (Tilak, Lal-Bal-Pal) distrusting the Moderates' constitutional methods. Option B is the direct, specific cause. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Surat Split 1907: Bal Gangadhar Tilak vs moderate Congress leadership on approach to British
Led to temporary dominance of Moderates in Congress; Extremists expelled
contrast_with: Moderates (petitions, constitutional means) vs Extremists (mass agitation, swaraj demand)

subject: History
trap_factor: Option A (complete independence) is an attractive answer as the 1942 period saw maximum pressure for independence, while option C (republic with Commonwealth) confuses it with actual 1949 outcome.
elimination_hint: Cripps Mission 1942 offered Dominion Status after WWII with right to secede from Commonwealth — neither full independence nor partition plan. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Cripps Mission proposed Dominion Status after WWII and constituent assembly for Indians
Congress rejected it calling it a 'post-dated cheque on a failing bank' (Gandhi)
contrast_with: Cripps (Dominion Status post-war) vs Cabinet Mission (Union of India with provinces)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Bodhgaya in Baghelkhand (S1) is the key trap — Bodhgaya is in Bihar's Magadh region, not Baghelkhand (MP/UP border). Shirdi in Vidarbha (S3) is also wrong — Shirdi is in Ahmednagar, not Vidarbha.
elimination_hint: Only Khajuraho (Bundelkhand) and Tirupati (Rayalaseema) are correctly matched. Bodhgaya=Magadh/Bihar; Shirdi=Ahmednagar; Nashik=Nashik division not Malwa. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Khajuraho is in Chhatarpur district, which falls in Bundelkhand region of MP
Tirupati is in Chittoor district of Rayalaseema region, Andhra Pradesh
contrast_with: Baghelkhand (SE MP) vs Bundelkhand (central MP/SW UP) — distinct regional identities

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates often confuse which House passes this resolution and the majority required. Lok Sabha resolutions and simple majority are both tempting wrong answers.
elimination_hint: Under Article 249, Rajya Sabha can authorize Parliament to legislate on State List subjects by a 2/3 majority of members present and voting. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Article 249: Rajya Sabha resolution by 2/3 majority allows Parliament to legislate on State List
Resolution valid for 1 year, renewable indefinitely
prerequisite_concepts: Rajya Sabha special role in federalism
contrast_with: Art 249 (Rajya Sabha 2/3) vs Art 250 (during National Emergency — automatic Parliament jurisdiction)
contemporary_relevance: Centre-state legislative relations remain relevant in cooperative/competitive federalism debates.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka both have coastal access needs; Chhattisgarh is landlocked and has discussed port access but less specifically than Rajasthan.
elimination_hint: Rajasthan (landlocked state) explored building an inland port near Jodhpur/Barmer to be connected to the sea via a navigational channel for trade connectivity. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Rajasthan is India's largest state by area but landlocked, hence explored artificial inland port
Connects to Gujarat ports via road/rail but wanted navigational channel access
contrast_with: Coastal states (direct port access) vs landlocked states (need special infrastructure)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the trap — SDGs were proposed at Rio+20 (2012) by UN member states, not in 1972 by Club of Rome (which published 'Limits to Growth' but did not propose SDGs).
elimination_hint: SDGs were adopted by the UN in September 2015 at the UNGA, to be achieved by 2030. Statement 1 is factually wrong. Answer: B (2 only).
factual_hooks: 17 SDGs adopted by UN in 2015 at UNGA, to be achieved by 2030
Club of Rome (1972) published 'Limits to Growth' — NOT the same as SDGs
contrast_with: MDGs (8 goals, 2000–2015, developing countries focus) vs SDGs (17 goals, 2015–2030, universal)
contemporary_relevance: SDG progress review critical as 2030 deadline approaches.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: S. Chandrasekhar and S.N. Bose are distinguished Indian scientists whose biographies could plausibly be subjects of biographical films, causing confusion.
elimination_hint: The Man Who Knew Infinity (2015 film) is specifically about Srinivasa Ramanujan and his collaboration with G.H. Hardy at Cambridge. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: S. Ramanujan (1887–1920) was a self-taught mathematical genius who made extraordinary contributions to number theory
Dev Patel played Ramanujan in the 2015 film
contrast_with: Ramanujan (pure mathematics) vs Chandrasekhar (astrophysics) — different domains of Indian scientific genius

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 (age 25) is the trap — Article 243F sets minimum age for Panchayat membership at 21, not 25. Candidates may confuse with age for Parliament (25 for Lok Sabha).
elimination_hint: Minimum age for Panchayat membership is 21 years (not 25). Statement 2 about remainder term is constitutionally correct (Art 243E). Answer: B (2 only).
factual_hooks: Article 243F: minimum age for Panchayat membership is 21 years
Prematurely dissolved Panchayat's successor completes only the remaining term
contrast_with: Panchayat member age (21) vs Lok Sabha MP age (25) vs Rajya Sabha age (30)
contemporary_relevance: Panchayati Raj institutions crucial for grassroots democracy and decentralization.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Choropleth map (shading by areal unit) is often confused with isopleth as both show distribution. Candidates mixing cartographic terminology may select A.
elimination_hint: Isobars connect points of equal atmospheric pressure — isopleth maps connect points of equal value. Isobar maps are a classic example of isopleth maps. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Isopleth maps connect points of equal value (isobars, isotherms, isohyets)
Choropleth maps use shading/color to show values by administrative unit
contrast_with: Isopleth (continuous data, equal-value lines) vs Choropleth (discrete areal units with shading)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Jawahar Rozgar Yojana and IRDP are both poverty-linked schemes that could plausibly include urban employment components. The specific urban micro-enterprise focus narrows it.
elimination_hint: Nehru Rozgar Yojana (1989) specifically included Urban Micro Enterprises, Urban Wage Employment, and Housing/Shelter upgradation for urban poor. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Nehru Rozgar Yojana (1989) targeted urban poverty and unemployment
Later subsumed into SJSRY (Swarna Jayanti Shahari Rozgar Yojana) in 1997
contrast_with: NRY (urban focus) vs JRY (rural focus) — different target populations

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Protectionism and trade wars (US-China) have disrupted global supply chains
India faces dual challenge: opportunity from China+1 strategy and risk from retaliatory tariffs
contrast_with: Multilateralism (WTO rules-based) vs bilateralism (direct country-to-country deals)
mains_themes: India's FTA strategy in bipolar world
mains_intro: The global shift from free trade to protectionism poses both structural challenges and strategic opportunities for India's economic integration.
mains_body_points: Challenges: Export market uncertainty, supply chain disruption, WTO dispute settlement weakening
Opportunity: China+1 strategy enabling India to attract manufacturing investment
India's FTA push (UAE, Australia, UK negotiations) as bilateral hedge against multilateral collapse
Need to upgrade domestic manufacturing competitiveness, reduce logistics costs
Strategic autonomy vs integration dilemma — India's cautious trade policy stance
mains_conclusion: India must pragmatically combine selective bilateral agreements, domestic industrial competitiveness, and strategic supply chain positioning to navigate the post-globalization trade landscape.
contemporary_relevance: Highly relevant given US tariff actions in 2025 and India's FTA negotiations.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates confuse the sequence of Southeast Asian capitals. Bangkok and Hong Kong are both commonly visited hubs and their order relative to Hanoi/Taipei is often mixed up.
elimination_hint: Delhi to Tokyo: going eastward, the sequence is Bangkok (IV) → Hanoi (II) → Hong Kong (I) → Taipei (III). Answer: B (IV, II, I, III).
factual_hooks: Bangkok is the westernmost Southeast Asian capital on this route; Taipei is closest to Japan
Hanoi (Vietnam) is between Bangkok and Hong Kong geographically
contrast_with: Eastern vs Western Southeast Asia — different route corridors

subject: History
trap_factor: Bishnupur (famous for terracotta temples and folk art) is commonly associated with West Bengal, but matching the others (Sisupalgarh=Orissa, Goalpara=Assam) requires precise knowledge.
elimination_hint: Sisupalgarh (Orissa/Odisha), Piprahwa (UP — Buddha's birthplace relics), Goalpara (Assam), Bishnupur (Manipur in this 2006 question context, though West Bengal Bishnupur is more famous). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Sisupalgarh is a protohistoric fortified city site in Odisha
Piprahwa in UP contains stupa relics believed to be associated with Buddha
contrast_with: Sisupalgarh (Odisha fortified city) vs Piprahwa (UP Buddhist stupa) — different types and periods

subject: Environment
factual_hooks: CCUS captures CO2 from industrial sources/atmosphere and stores it underground or uses it as feedstock
Can achieve 'negative emissions' when combined with bioenergy (BECCS)
contrast_with: CCUS (capture and use/store) vs Carbon offset (compensate via forests/sinks)
mains_themes: Role of technology in climate mitigation
mains_intro: Carbon Capture, Utilization and Storage (CCUS) represents a critical bridging technology to manage CO2 emissions while transitioning to clean energy.
mains_body_points: CCUS process: capture CO2 at source → compress → transport → geological storage or industrial utilization
Applications: cement, steel, chemical industries where direct electrification is difficult
Utilization pathways: converting CO2 to synthetic fuels, building materials, chemicals
Potential for negative emissions via BECCS (Bioenergy + CCS)
Challenges: high costs, energy penalty, storage site verification, scale-up requirements
India context: CCUS relevant for coal-dependent economy during transition
mains_conclusion: While CCUS is not a substitute for emissions reduction, it offers a viable pathway for decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors and achieving net-zero targets by 2070.
contemporary_relevance: Central to India's net zero 2070 commitment and industrial decarbonization strategy.

subject: History
trap_factor: Matching artists with art forms requires specific knowledge — Malini Rajurkar (Hindustani vocal) is often confused with Kuchipudi/Bharatanatyam, and Hiren Bhattacharya (puppetry) is obscure.
elimination_hint: Hiren Bhattacharya was known for puppetry (A-4); Malini Rajurkar for Hindustani vocal (B-2); Pratibha Prahlad for Bharatanatyam (C-1); Vempati Chinna Satyam for Kuchipudi (D-3). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Vempati Chinna Satyam (1929–2012) was a legendary Kuchipudi master who popularized it as solo female dance
Malini Rajurkar is a renowned Jaipur-Atrauli gharana Hindustani vocalist
contrast_with: Kuchipudi (from Krishna district, Andhra) vs Bharatanatyam (from Tamil Nadu) — both classical but different origins

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Reverse osmosis (used in water purifiers) and dialysis (kidney function) are commonly encountered in everyday context, tempting candidates away from centrifugation.
elimination_hint: Washing machine spin-dryer uses centrifugal force to extract water from clothes — centrifugation. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Centrifugation: rotation generates centrifugal force separating components by density
Dialysis separates particles by membrane diffusion (kidney/water purification context)
contrast_with: Centrifugation (force-based separation) vs dialysis (membrane-based diffusion separation)

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Fiscal Health Index (FHI) assesses states on revenue mobilization, expenditure quality, debt sustainability
Launched by Finance Ministry to encourage transparent state fiscal management
contrast_with: FHI (comprehensive index) vs FRBM compliance (single-parameter rule-based approach)
mains_intro: The Fiscal Health Index serves as a multi-dimensional diagnostic tool enabling evidence-based assessment and comparison of state fiscal performance.
mains_body_points: FHI parameters: revenue mobilization, expenditure quality, fiscal prudence, debt sustainability, debt service efficiency
Creates competitive benchmarking among states — encouraging fiscal discipline through transparency
Can inform Finance Commission's fiscal transfers and grants — linking performance to incentives
Encourages states to reduce revenue deficits, avoid off-budget borrowings, improve capital expenditure quality
Limitations: index may not capture welfare expenditure adequacy or state-specific development contexts
Complementary to FRBM Act — adds qualitative assessment to rule-based compliance framework
mains_conclusion: The Fiscal Health Index, by making state fiscal performance transparent and comparable, has potential to shift state incentives toward sustainable fiscal management while respecting federal autonomy.
contemporary_relevance: Highly relevant given rising state debt concerns and post-COVID fiscal consolidation challenges.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Menthol (from peppermint) and Benzaldehyde (almond smell) are aromatic compounds that candidates may associate with cloves. Methanol sounds similar but is an alcohol, not an aromatic compound.
elimination_hint: Eugenol is the primary component (70–90%) of clove oil, responsible for its characteristic aroma and analgesic properties used in dentistry. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Eugenol (C10H12O2) is the active component in clove oil with antiseptic and analgesic properties
Used in dentistry as a temporary dental cement and pain reliever
contrast_with: Eugenol (cloves) vs Menthol (mint) — different plant-derived aromatic compounds

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Iron is the classic example of ferromagnetic (not paramagnetic) material. Candidates often confuse ferromagnetism with paramagnetism. Hydrogen and Nitrogen are diamagnetic.
elimination_hint: Oxygen (O2) is paramagnetic due to two unpaired electrons in its molecular orbitals — a well-known exception among diatomic molecules. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: O2 is paramagnetic — it has 2 unpaired electrons in anti-bonding π* orbitals
Iron is ferromagnetic (strong permanent magnetism), not merely paramagnetic
contrast_with: Paramagnetic (O2 — weak, temporary) vs Ferromagnetic (Fe — strong, permanent) magnetic behavior

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Option A (Blood → Lungs → Tissue) is tempting as it reverses the actual flow. Some candidates confuse inspiration with oxygen transport sequence.
elimination_hint: Oxygen enters via lungs (II) → binds to hemoglobin in blood (I) → delivered to tissues (III). Sequence: Lungs → Blood → Tissue = II, I, III. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Oxygen transport: Lungs (gas exchange) → Blood (hemoglobin binding) → Tissue (cellular respiration)
Hemoglobin carries ~98% of oxygen in blood; 2% dissolved in plasma
contrast_with: Oxygen transport (lungs→blood→tissue) vs CO2 removal (tissue→blood→lungs) — reverse pathway

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Pancreas (hormone producing) is tempting as corpus luteum produces progesterone; brain (pituitary) controls reproductive hormones which further confuses location.
elimination_hint: Corpus luteum is formed from the ruptured Graafian follicle after ovulation — it is located in the ovary. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Corpus luteum forms after ovulation in the ovary and secretes progesterone
Degenerates if fertilization doesn't occur; maintained by hCG if pregnancy occurs
contrast_with: Corpus luteum (post-ovulation progesterone) vs Corpus albicans (degenerated corpus luteum)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Filaria is commonly confused with lymph nodes vs muscle; Leukaemia candidates may misidentify as blood cells instead of bone marrow (where blood cells are produced).
elimination_hint: Malaria=blood cells(E); Filaria=lymph node(D); Encephalitis=brain(B); Leukaemia=bone marrow(A). Answer: B (I-E, II-D, III-B, IV-A).
factual_hooks: Leukaemia originates in bone marrow where abnormal WBC production occurs
Filaria (lymphatic filariasis) affects lymph nodes causing lymphedema/elephantiasis
contrast_with: Malaria (RBC destruction) vs Leukaemia (WBC overproduction) — both blood-related but different mechanisms

subject: Science & Technology
factual_hooks: Nanotechnology operates at 1–100 nanometer scale enabling precise material engineering
Nano-pesticides, nano-fertilizers, and nano-sensors can transform agricultural efficiency
contrast_with: Nano-fertilizers (targeted, efficient) vs conventional fertilizers (broadcast, wasteful)
mains_intro: Nanotechnology offers a paradigm shift in agriculture by enabling precision delivery of inputs, real-time monitoring, and improved crop protection at nanoscale.
mains_body_points: Nano-fertilizers: slow-release nanoparticles improve nutrient use efficiency, reduce soil degradation
Nano-pesticides: targeted delivery reduces chemical runoff, environmental harm, and resistance
Nano-sensors: real-time monitoring of soil health, moisture, pathogens for precision farming
Plant nano-bionics: enhancing photosynthesis efficiency using carbon nanotubes
Socio-economic impact: reduced input costs improve farmer margins and land productivity
Risks: nano-particle toxicity, environmental persistence, regulatory gaps need attention
mains_conclusion: By enabling precision agriculture with reduced inputs and higher yields, nanotechnology holds transformative potential to improve both agricultural sustainability and farmers' economic wellbeing.
contemporary_relevance: Relevant to India's precision farming push and doubling farmer income goals.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may attempt to find QP distance instead of QR. The midpoint calculation requires finding coordinates first, then applying distance formula.
factual_hooks: Midpoint formula: R = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2)
Distance formula: d = √[(x2-x1)²+(y2-y1)²]

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse volume (a³) with surface area (6a²) formulas or set up the equation incorrectly as a³=6a instead of a³=6a².
elimination_hint: Volume = a³; Surface Area = 6a². If a³ = 6a², then a = 6. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Cube: Volume = a³, Surface Area = 6a²
When numerically equal: a³ = 6a² → a = 6

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The complex constraints (V>P>Q in order, and R first+T last OR S first+U/Q last) create a confusing tree of possibilities when V is fixed at 5th.
elimination_hint: With V at 5th: P and Q must finish after V (6th/7th). Since T cannot be last (R must be first if T is last, but R can't be 2nd-4th with P, Q needing spots), S must be first. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Logical deduction with ordered sequences requires eliminating impossible arrangements systematically
Fixed position of V(5th) forces P and Q to 6th/7th, triggering S-first rule

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The three rounds of transfers create a cumulative state that is hard to track mentally; candidates may lose track of the current position after each round of swaps.
elimination_hint: Initial: S-P, D-Q, J-R, K-S. After 1972: S-R, J-P, D-S, K-Q. After 1973: J-S, D-P, S-Q, K-R. Need everyone at all 4 places: swap D&K and S&J (option C). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Track swap outcomes step-by-step: build a position matrix for each year
After 3 rounds, identify which pairs need to swap to complete the cycle

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may incorrectly add all values (6+15+6=27) then simply subtract the bilingual (2) people without properly accounting for the trilingual person being included in both.
elimination_hint: Total = Tamil(6) + Hindi(15) + Gujarati(6) − bilingual overlaps(2) − 2×trilingual(1) + trilingual(1) = 27 − 2 − 2 + 1 = 24. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Inclusion-exclusion: |A∪B∪C| = |A|+|B|+|C|-|A∩B|-|B∩C|-|A∩C|+|A∩B∩C|
Trilingual person counted 3 times in individual totals, must subtract 2 extra counts

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the classic trap — prorogation does NOT cause Bills to lapse (only dissolution of Lok Sabha does). Candidates confuse prorogation with dissolution.
elimination_hint: S1 is wrong — Bills don't lapse on prorogation, only on dissolution of Lok Sabha. S2 is correct — Rajya Sabha bills pending before Lok Sabha passes them don't lapse on LS dissolution. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Prorogation ends a session; dissolution ends the Lok Sabha term — only dissolution causes bill lapse
Bills pending in Rajya Sabha (passed by RS but not LS) don't lapse on LS dissolution
contrast_with: Prorogation (session ends, bills preserved) vs Dissolution (Lok Sabha ends, most bills lapse)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Child wasting is often confused with child stunting. GHI now uses child wasting (not stunting alone) but earlier versions varied; candidates must recall current methodology.
elimination_hint: GHI 2021 onwards uses: undernourishment + child stunting + child wasting + child mortality (4 indicators). But in 2016 context: undernourishment, child stunting, child wasting, child mortality. The question lists stunting not wasting — historically all three (1, 2, 3) were included. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: GHI is calculated by IFPRI using undernourishment, child stunting, child wasting, and child mortality
India consistently ranks poorly on GHI due to high child malnutrition rates
contrast_with: Stunting (chronic malnutrition, height) vs Wasting (acute malnutrition, weight) — different malnutrition types
contemporary_relevance: India's GHI rank controversy (2022–23) remains relevant.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option D (all four) is tempting but reducing import duty actually increases imports and widens trade deficit. Option B (new welfare schemes) clearly increases expenditure.
elimination_hint: Reducing revenue expenditure (S1) and rationalizing subsidies (S3) both directly cut government spending to reduce deficit. New welfare schemes increase deficit; reducing import duty increases imports. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Budget deficit = Revenue Expenditure + Capital Expenditure - Total Receipts
Subsidies rationalization and revenue expenditure cuts are the primary fiscal consolidation tools
contrast_with: Revenue deficit (recurrent spending > revenue) vs Fiscal deficit (total spending > total receipts)
contemporary_relevance: Fiscal deficit management critical post-COVID with high debt-to-GDP ratio.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 2 is the trap — Payment Banks can issue prepaid cards/debit cards but NOT credit cards. The distinction between debit and credit card issuance is the key trick.
elimination_hint: Payment Banks can be promoted by telecom/supermarket companies (S1 correct). They cannot issue credit cards (S2 wrong). They cannot lend (S3 correct). Answer: B (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Payment Banks have limited mandate: accept deposits up to ₹2 lakh, no lending, no credit cards
Paytm Payments Bank, Airtel Payments Bank are prominent examples
contrast_with: Payment Banks (no credit, limited deposits) vs Small Finance Banks (can lend to small borrowers)
contemporary_relevance: Payment Banks' viability concerns following Paytm Payments Bank restrictions.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The question asks which TWO statements can both be TRUE but cannot both be FALSE — candidates must test all pairs for simultaneous truth and mutual falsity.
elimination_hint: I (all black) and IV (no black) cannot both be true; but if some are white (not all black, not all white), I is false AND IV is false simultaneously. Test: I&IV fail. I(all black) true means IV false; I false doesn't force IV true. When can I&IV both be false? When 'only some are black' — YES, both I and IV are false. Not I&IV. Check III&IV: both false means 'not only some black' AND 'not no black' = all black OR all white. Then I or II true. III&IV can both be false. I&II: both true = all black AND all white = impossible. I&II can't both be true. Best pair: I&IV cannot BOTH be false (if I false→III true, IV may be true/false). Actually: I and IV — I=all black, IV=no black. These are contraries — can't both be true. Can both be false? Yes (if some black, some white). So I&IV are NOT the answer. Rechecking: the answer is A (I and IV) per UPSC key — meaning they CAN both be true is impossible but CANNOT both be false means exactly one must hold? Actually in strict logic, I and IV are contradictories of each other only through III. Standard answer: A.
factual_hooks: Logical contraries: 'All X are Y' and 'No X are Y' cannot both be true but can both be false
The correct pair that can't both be false while can't both be true requires careful logical analysis

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Both statements about LiFi are essentially correct — it uses light (visible/infrared) for data transmission and is faster than WiFi. No significant trap here.
elimination_hint: LiFi (Light Fidelity) uses visible light for data transmission (S1 correct) and is indeed faster than WiFi theoretically (S2 correct). Answer: C (Both).
factual_hooks: LiFi uses LED light fluctuations for data transmission at speeds up to 224 Gbps
LiFi cannot pass through walls — high security but limited range compared to WiFi
contrast_with: LiFi (light, high speed, no penetration) vs WiFi (radio waves, moderate speed, wall penetration)
contemporary_relevance: LiFi development relevant to next-generation wireless communication infrastructure.

subject: Polity
factual_hooks: Article 19(1)(a) guarantees freedom of speech and expression; subject to reasonable restrictions under 19(2)
Films subject to pre-censorship via CBFC — no equivalent exists for print/speech
contrast_with: Speech/print (post-publication restrictions) vs films (pre-censorship certification) — different regulatory frameworks
mains_themes: Freedom of speech vs public order
mains_intro: Freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) is a cornerstone of democracy but must be balanced with constitutionally recognized restrictions.
mains_body_points: Scope: covers press freedom, commercial speech, silence, symbolic speech, right to know
Limitations under Art 19(2): sovereignty, security, friendly relations, public order, decency, morality, contempt, defamation
Hate speech: not explicitly defined in Indian law; IPC 153A/295A/505 address hate speech partially
Films uniquely subject to pre-censorship via CBFC under Cinematograph Act — unlike books, press, or speeches
Rationale for special film treatment: mass medium, visual impact, potential for widespread emotive reaction
Recent debates: OTT regulation, social media speech, cartoons/satire vs sedition
mains_conclusion: While freedom of speech is fundamental, India's framework must evolve to clearly codify hate speech laws while ensuring film certification moves from censorship to classification.
contemporary_relevance: Highly relevant given debates on social media regulation, CBFC decisions, and hate speech cases.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Option D (SDGs) is a strong trap since INDCs/NDCs are part of the same UN sustainable development discourse. Option A (refugee pledges) distracts with the 2015 Syria crisis context.
elimination_hint: INDCs (now NDCs) are national climate action pledges submitted under the Paris Agreement framework to combat climate change. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: INDCs submitted before Paris COP21 2015; became NDCs after Paris Agreement ratification
India's INDC: 40% clean energy by 2030, 33–35% emissions intensity reduction from 2005 levels
contrast_with: INDC/NDC (national pledges) vs Carbon Budget (globally allocated emission space)
contemporary_relevance: NDC updates critical as Paris Agreement midpoint review approaches.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option B (electricity to every household — SAUBHAGYA scheme) is easily confused with UDAY as both relate to electricity sector. Option A (renewables/startups) is also plausible.
elimination_hint: UDAY = Ujwal DISCOM Assurance Yojana — specifically for financial turnaround of electricity distribution companies (DISCOMs) by reducing their debt burden. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: UDAY (2015) helped state DISCOMs transfer debt to state governments at lower interest
Aimed to reduce AT&C (Aggregate Technical and Commercial) losses in power distribution
contrast_with: UDAY (DISCOM financial revival) vs SAUBHAGYA (household electrification coverage) — same sector, different objectives
contemporary_relevance: DISCOM financial health remains critical for India's power sector viability.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may doubt S1 as IFC being a World Bank arm is not always recalled; or doubt S2 as Masala Bonds denomination (rupee) is a key distinctive feature worth verifying.
elimination_hint: IFC is indeed the private sector arm of the World Bank Group (S1 correct). Masala Bonds are rupee-denominated bonds issued in overseas markets (S2 correct). Answer: C (Both).
factual_hooks: Masala Bonds: rupee-denominated bonds issued in overseas markets — exchange rate risk on investor
IFC was first to issue Masala Bonds in 2014 to raise rupee funds for Indian projects
contrast_with: Masala Bonds (rupee-denominated, currency risk on buyer) vs ECB (foreign currency denominated, risk on Indian borrower)
contemporary_relevance: Internationalization of rupee through such instruments remains a policy goal.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may select A (sealed tins always contain perishable food) which reverses the logical implication, or D which introduces a claim about non-perishable foods not in the premises.
elimination_hint: From S1 (processed meat is perishable) and S2 (all perishable foods packed in sealed tins): processed meat must always be packed in sealed tins. S3 doesn't contradict this. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Deductive syllogism: if All A→B and X is A, then X→B
Statement III is a non-sequitur and doesn't change the logical conclusion about processed meat

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Punjab and Haryana are both densely connected states; candidates may swap their order. Tamil Nadu's dense road network in the South surprises many candidates who expect northern states to dominate.
elimination_hint: Road density (per 100 km²) ranking: Tamil Nadu (IV) has highest dense road network, followed by Maharashtra (II), Punjab (III), Haryana (I). Answer: A (IV, II, III, I).
factual_hooks: Tamil Nadu has one of India's highest road densities due to dense urban-rural network
Road density = total road length / area × 100
contrast_with: Road density (length per area) vs road connectivity (percentage of villages with roads) — different metrics

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may choose Fundamental Rights (B) as they are the most enforceable expression of constitutional values, or DPSP (C) as they guide state policy — both are cited as reflecting makers' intent.
elimination_hint: The Preamble is often called the 'soul of the Constitution' and best reflects the framers' vision and intent. Supreme Court in Berubari Union case noted this. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Preamble declares India as Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic with Justice, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity
Berubari Union Case (1960): Preamble is part of Constitution but not enforceable; Kesavananda Bharati (1973) confirmed it's part of Constitution
contrast_with: Preamble (aspirational, interpretive) vs Fundamental Rights (justiciable, enforceable)
contemporary_relevance: Preamble values debated in context of religious freedom, secularism cases.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Bhavnagar (Gulf of Khambhat/Cambay) is a strong distractor as it has among India's highest tidal range. The question describes walking on the sea floor during low tide — extreme tidal range phenomenon.
elimination_hint: Chandipur (Odisha) is famous for its unique phenomenon where sea recedes 5 km during low tide, allowing people to walk on the sea floor. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Chandipur-on-Sea, Odisha: sea recedes up to 5 km at low tide twice daily — unique phenomenon
The beach is also a missile testing area (ITR — Integrated Test Range)
contrast_with: Chandipur (sea recedes, walk on floor) vs Bhavnagar/Gulf of Khambhat (highest tidal range but different phenomenon)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 (fungal infections) is the trap — butterflies are pollinators and predators of some insects, but their decline doesn't directly cause fungal infections in plants. This is a distant/unestablished ecological link.
elimination_hint: Butterfly decline affects pollination (S1 correct) and disrupts food chains harming wasps, spiders, birds that feed on butterflies (S3 correct). S2 (fungal infections) lacks direct causal link. Answer: C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Butterflies pollinate 75+ plant species; their decline directly reduces pollination success
Butterflies are prey for spiders, wasps, and insectivorous birds — their decline cascades up food chain
contrast_with: Butterflies (pollinators + prey) vs Bees (primarily pollinators) — different ecological roles
contemporary_relevance: Pollinator decline linked to pesticide use and habitat loss is a global conservation concern.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statements 3 and 4 (millets consumption and poultry eggs) are the traps — National Nutrition Mission focuses on awareness, anaemia, and malnutrition reduction, not specific dietary promotion of millets or eggs.
elimination_hint: National Nutrition Mission (POSHAN Abhiyan) focuses on awareness about malnutrition (S1) and reducing anaemia (S2). It does not specifically promote millets or poultry eggs as stated. Answer: A (1 and 2 only).
factual_hooks: POSHAN Abhiyan/National Nutrition Mission targets reducing stunting, wasting, anaemia, and low birth weight
Launched 2018 with target to reduce stunting by 2% and anaemia by 3% per year
contrast_with: NNM (awareness + anaemia focus) vs ICDS (supplementary nutrition delivery for mothers/children)
contemporary_relevance: Malnutrition remains a critical challenge for India's human development index.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 mentions DIPP (now DPIIT) as nodal agency — this is correct. Statement 1 about Doha Development Agenda commitment may be doubted by candidates unsure about India's TRIPS stance.
elimination_hint: India's National IPR Policy (2016) reiterates commitment to Doha Development Agenda and TRIPS (S1 correct). DIPP (now DPIIT) is the nodal agency (S2 correct). Answer: C (Both).
factual_hooks: India's National IPR Policy 2016: 'Creative India; Innovative India' — balanced innovation with public interest
DPIIT (formerly DIPP) is the nodal department for India's IPR regime
contrast_with: TRIPS (minimum IP standards) vs Doha Declaration (public health flexibilities) — tension in IPR policy
contemporary_relevance: IPR policy crucial for pharma innovation vs access to medicines debate.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 1 sounds plausible if candidates recall an India-housing conference; Statement 2 about ADB/APEC/ASEAN partnership is a clear overstatement that should trigger suspicion.
elimination_hint: APMCHUD first conference was in New Delhi 2006 (S1 correct). India does NOT host all Annual Ministerial Conferences — they rotate among Asia-Pacific countries (S2 wrong). Answer: A (1 only).
factual_hooks: APMCHUD first ministerial conference was in New Delhi 2006 on urban housing
APMCHUD was established under UN-HABITAT's Asia-Pacific operations
contrast_with: APMCHUD (regional ministerial forum) vs UN-HABITAT (global body for human settlements)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 says genome sequencing identifies markers for disease resistance AND drought tolerance — both are valid. Statement 2 about reducing breeding time is valid. Statement 3 about host-pathogen relationships is also valid. All three are legitimate applications.
elimination_hint: All three statements are valid applications of genome sequencing in agriculture: disease/drought markers (S1), faster breeding (S2), and host-pathogen dynamics (S3). Answer: D (all three).
factual_hooks: Genome sequencing in agriculture enables marker-assisted selection, reducing traditional breeding timelines significantly
Rice, wheat, and chickpea genomes have been sequenced for crop improvement in India
contrast_with: Traditional breeding (years, phenotype selection) vs Genome-assisted breeding (faster, genotype-based)
contemporary_relevance: Genome sequencing critical for developing climate-resilient crop varieties.
